question_title: Will Susan Collins lead the Democratic candidate in the Maine Senate race polling on August 31, 2026?
summary: The question resolves YES if Collins leads the Democratic nominee in the RaceToTheWH polling average on Aug 31, 2026. The race was essentially tied against Graham Platner, who withdrew July 10 amid scandal. Democrats select a new nominee ~July 27, leaving ~5 weeks for fresh polls. A newly-minted, lower-recognition Democrat typically trails or ties a long-time incumbent like Collins in initial polling, and the recent trend was already tilting toward Collins (Fox 50-47; Polymarket moved to ~63.5% GOP). Counterweights: Maine's Democratic federal lean, a 2026 midterm out-party tailwind, generic Democrats polling competitively (UMass Lowell had Dem +5), and the likely nominee (Troy Jackson) being a recognizable former Senate President. There is also material uncertainty about whether fresh polls exist by Aug 31 and how RaceToTheWH handles the matchup transition — if a stale Dem-leaning Platner average persists, it resolves NO. Net: modestly favors Collins leading, ~0.60.

factors.description: Pre-withdrawal polling average roughly tied with recent tightening toward Collins (Fox 50-47 Collins, Siena 49-47 Platner).
New Democratic nominee (chosen ~July 27) will have lower name recognition than incumbent Collins; fresh challengers typically trail or tie an established incumbent in early polls.
Polymarket moved to ~63.5% for the Republican/Collins side after Platner's exit (winner market, indirect signal).
Maine leans Democratic federally and 2026 is a midterm with Trump in office (out-party tailwind); generic Democrat polls competitively (UMass Lowell had Dem +5).
Uncertainty about whether fresh polls exist by Aug 31 and how RaceToTheWH handles the transition; a stale Dem-leaning Platner average would resolve NO.
Likely nominee Troy Jackson is a former statewide Senate President with meaningful name recognition, weakening the 'unknown challenger' advantage for Collins.

reasoning:
My forecast: **0.60** (60% probability that Susan Collins leads the Democratic candidate in the RaceToTheWH polling average on August 31, 2026).

**Key reasoning:**
- The race was essentially tied against Graham Platner (Dem +1 to R+1), with the most recent polls trending toward Collins (Fox 50-47).
- Platner withdrew July 10; a new nominee is selected only ~July 27, leaving ~5 weeks for fresh polls. A newly-minted, lower-recognition Democrat typically trails or ties a long-time incumbent in early head-to-head polling — the strongest factor favoring YES.
- Polymarket shifted to ~63.5% for the Republican side after the withdrawal.

**Counterweights keeping this off a high number:**
- Maine's Democratic federal lean and a favorable 2026 midterm environment for Democrats
- Generic Democrats have polled well against Collins historically
- The likely nominee (Troy Jackson) is a recognizable statewide figure
- Genuine uncertainty about whether the RaceToTheWH tracker will have fresh polls by Aug 31 or carry a stale, Dem-leaning Platner average (which would resolve NO)

The premortem reviewer independently estimated 0.55-0.62, consistent with my forecast.
resolution_criteria: This question resolves as **Yes** if on August 31, 2026, Susan Collins leads the Democratic nominee in the General Election Polling Average at the [2026 Senate Polling](https://www.racetothewh.com/senate/26) tracker at the RaceToTheWH website.
fine_print: INSTRUCTIONS: At the resolution link, scroll down to Polling Average. Then select the race under Choose a State & Matchup.

factors.description: Pre-withdrawal polling average was roughly tied (Dem +1 to R+1), with the most recent polls tightening toward Collins (Fox 50-47 Collins, Siena 49-47 Platner). Starting point near 50/50 but slightly favoring Collins recently.

New Democratic nominee (chosen ~July 27) will have lower name recognition than incumbent Collins. Fresh challengers typically trail or tie an established incumbent in initial head-to-head polls 5 weeks after nomination.
Polymarket moved to ~63.5% for the Republican/Collins side after Platner's exit, reflecting perceived Democratic disarray.

Maine leans Democratic in federal races and 2026 is a midterm with Trump as president (out-party tailwind); generic Democrat polls competitively against Collins (UMass Lowell had Platner +5). Could keep Democrat ahead in polling average.
Uncertainty about whether fresh polls for the new matchup exist by Aug 31 and how RaceToTheWH handles the transition; if stale Platner average persists it was roughly tied.
Troy Jackson (likely nominee) is a former statewide Senate President with meaningful name recognition, weakening the pure 'unknown challenger' advantage for Collins.

input.anchor: Race was ~tied (Dem +1) pre-withdrawal; incumbent-vs-fresh-challenger dynamics and recent tightening toward Collins push the anchor slightly above 50% for Collins leading.
input.assessment: The question resolves YES if Collins leads the Democratic nominee in the RaceToTheWH polling average on Aug 31, 2026. The race was essentially tied against Platner, with a recent trend toward Collins. Platner's withdrawal creates a new, likely lesser-known nominee who will be set only ~5 weeks before resolution. Fresh challengers generally start behind or even with well-known incumbents, favoring Collins leading the polling average. Counterweights: Maine's Democratic federal lean, a favorable midterm environment for Democrats, and the possibility the nominee (e.g., Troy Jackson) has decent recognition. Net: modestly favors Collins leading, ~0.60-0.65.
input.key_uncertainties: Whether fresh polls for the new matchup are published by Aug 31; identity and recognition of the new nominee; RaceToTheWH's handling of the transition; direction of house effects in new polls.
input.tool_audit: research() returned a comprehensive, well-sourced summary with polling data, the withdrawal news, and market signals. Did not directly query the RaceToTheWH tracker's current average, but cross-referenced 270toWin and Pollsmax which are equivalent. Sufficient for this forecast.
input.process_reflection: The research tool was highly effective here, surfacing the critical, fast-moving development (Platner's withdrawal) that completely reshapes the forecast. The main residual uncertainty is mechanical: how RaceToTheWH's polling average behaves during a candidate transition with sparse fresh polls. A tool to directly fetch the current state of a specific poll tracker page would have reduced this uncertainty.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
I verified all six factors against the research trace. No hallucinations, no double-counting, no wrong-direction factors, and the assessment is internally consistent with the factors.

FACTOR GROUNDING (all confirmed in trace):
- F1: Fox News 6/30 Collins 50-47 and Siena 6/29 Platner 49-47 are exactly as reported (trace lines 682-684). "Roughly tied, tightening toward Collins" is accurate — 270toWin 5-poll avg was Platner +1.0, but pollsmax weighted (7/12) was R+0.9 and Fox was Collins +3, so recent trend genuinely favors Collins. Grounded.
- F2: New nominee chosen ~July 27, lower name recognition — grounded (convention July 25-26, deadline July 27).
- F3: Polymarket ~63.5% — grounded (trace lines 210-218). Correctly caveated as a WINNER market (November outcome), not a polling-lead market, so appropriately given modest weight.
- F4: UMass Lowell Platner +5 (48-43) — exactly matches trace line 1068. Maine Dem lean / midterm tailwind grounded.
- F5: Uncertainty about fresh polls and how RaceToTheWH handles the transition — grounded in the research caveats.
- F6: Troy Jackson former Senate President, frontrunner — grounded (trace lines 885, 899).

RESOLUTION-SOURCE GAP (basis for warn): Resolution criteria specify the RaceToTheWH tracker's General Election Polling Average as authoritative. The agent searched for it (trace line 962) but got no direct RaceToTheWH data back, and substituted 270toWin/Pollsmax as equivalents. It never retrieved the actual resolution source. This matters because RaceToTheWH may weight polls and handle the Platner->new-nominee transition differently than the aggregators the agent used — and Factor 5 acknowledges the average could even retain the stale Platner line (Dem +1), which would resolve NO. The agent is honest about this in its tool_audit and engages with the transition mechanics, which mitigates but does not eliminate the gap. This is at least a warn.

PROBABILITY: My independent estimate is ~0.55-0.62, consistent with the agent's 0.62. The pre-withdrawal race was a genuine tossup with a recent Collins-ward drift; a fresh, lesser-known Democrat set only ~5 weeks before resolution modestly favors Collins leading the average, offset by Maine's Dem federal lean, the midterm out-party tailwind, and the meaningful chance the aggregator carries a stale Dem-leaning Platner average. No meaningful divergence from the agent — not a fail.

COUNTERARGUMENT: Strong and genuine — constructs a coherent NO story (stale-average retention, Democratic lean, Jackson's real name recognition), not a token restatement. Self-confidence of 0.55 is appropriately calibrated given the unknown nominee and uncertain resolution-source behavior.

TOOL AUDIT: The agent's narrative ("comprehensive, well-sourced summary") is accurate and does not falsely claim zero failures, so it does not contradict the ground-truth metrics (research 50% error rate reflects one schema-validation failure retried successfully; several 403 WebFetch failures also occurred but were routed around). Minor omission, not a misrepresentation.

Net: well-reasoned, well-grounded forecast; the only real issue is the failure to retrieve the authoritative RaceToTheWH resolution source, whose transition-handling is a material unknown. Warn.
